title: Capital One releases VulnHunter, an open-source AI tool that finds software flaws before hackers do
blurb: Capital One released VulnHunter, an open-source agentic AI tool that scans source code for exploitable vulnerabilities, maps the attacker's path to each one, and proposes targeted fixes. Its "attacker-first" analysis reasons forward from real entry points like APIs and file uploads, while a built-in falsification engine discards findings it can disprove, cutting the false positives that bury conventional scanners.

title: Google-backed satellites for wildfire detection launch as smoke chokes US, Canada
blurb: The first three operational satellites in the Google-backed FireSat program launched aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 on July 7, marking the first constellation purpose-built to detect wildfires. After a three-month test period, the satellites will cover every fire-prone region twice daily and spot blazes as small as five by five meters through smoke and clouds, with fire agencies in California, Colorado, Australia, and Portugal as early adopters.

title: Kimi K3, and what we can still learn from the pelican benchmark
blurb: Moonshot AI announced Kimi K3, a 2.8T-parameter model it calls the first "open 3T-class" release, with open weights promised by July 27 and self-reported benchmarks beating Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 while trailing Claude Fable 5. At $3/$15 per million input/output tokens it is the priciest Chinese model yet, and Simon Willison's pelican-on-a-bicycle SVG test cost 25 cents to run.

title: The White House is dictating access to frontier AI models, shifting power from tech giants, sources say
blurb: The Trump administration is now dictating which companies can access frontier AI models from Anthropic and OpenAI, a decision that until now rested with the labs themselves, sources told CNBC. The shift follows last month's temporary block on Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5 over national security concerns, and comes as cheaper Chinese open-weight models like Moonshot's Kimi K3 close the performance gap with US frontier labs.

title: Sources: SpaceX is in talks with the DOD about providing the agency with access to data-center capacity worth billions of dollars for running AI models (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: SpaceX is in talks with the Department of Defense to provide the agency with billions of dollars in data-center capacity for running AI models, according to the Wall Street Journal. The deal would push Elon Musk's company deeper into government AI infrastructure, expanding its Pentagon footprint beyond launch and Starlink satellite communications.

title: Anthropic, Meta reportedly discussing $10B data center leasing deal
blurb: Anthropic is in early talks to lease roughly $10B of Meta's data center capacity over two years, according to three sources cited by The New York Times. The deal, worth about $416M per month, would follow Anthropic's $1.25B-per-month SpaceX supercomputer lease and pairs two direct rivals in the LLM market, giving Meta a marquee customer to offset its heavy infrastructure spending.

title: Overtraining as the path to human-like AI
blurb: Gwern, one of the earliest people to anticipate LLM scaling, published a 13,000-word essay arguing that overtraining models past the point where learning appears to stall is the path to flexible, human-like intelligence. The theory builds on "grokking," where regularization pressure eventually forces a model to abandon rote memorization for simpler, more general algorithms, and the author contends the analogy to human brains isn't load-bearing to the argument.

title: What Is Moonshot AI? Why China’s New Model Is Roiling Markets
blurb: Chinese startup Moonshot AI released a new AI model on July 17 that drew global attention for its benchmark performance and triggered sharp selloffs across equity markets. The launch renews concerns that low-cost Chinese frontier models can match US labs, pressuring valuations built on the assumption that American AI leadership stays durable and expensive.

title: New wp2shell WordPress Core Flaw Lets Unauthenticated Attackers Run Code
blurb: WordPress shipped 6.9.5 and 7.0.2 on July 17 to close a pre-auth RCE in core that an anonymous HTTP request can trigger against a default install with no plugins, affecting versions 6.9.0 through 6.9.4 and 7.0.0 through 7.0.1. Assetnote's Adam Kues reported the batch-route confusion and SQL injection flaw, dubbed wp2shell, and has withheld technical details while offering a checker at wp2shell.com.

title: Databricks hits $188B valuation, extending its run as AI’s favorite second act
blurb: Databricks raised a new round led by Coatue that values the company at $188B, up from $134B in February. The reported ~$3B raise caps an 18-month fundraising run as Databricks repositioned from big-data SaaS vendor to enterprise AI provider, shipping products like the Lakebase agent database and championing cheaper Chinese open-weight models for cost control.

title: Sources: the Trump administration is considering plans for an independent regulator to vet the safety of AI models; the regulator would report to the SEC (Bloomberg)
blurb: The Trump administration is weighing plans for an independent regulator to vet the safety of AI models, with the new body reporting to the SEC, according to Bloomberg. The structure would fold AI oversight into the securities regulator rather than creating a standalone agency, giving the effort existing enforcement muscle while leaving open questions about scope, mandatory testing, and which labs would fall under review.

title: How Apple’s big lawsuit could disrupt OpenAI’s IPO plans
blurb: Apple filed a trade secrets lawsuit against OpenAI last Friday, alleging a pattern of misconduct reaching up to OpenAI's chief hardware officer and claiming more than 400 former Apple employees now work there. The timing is difficult for OpenAI, which is reportedly targeting an IPO as early as this year while also building out its own hardware ambitions.

title: OpenAI’s new hardware is a $230, 13-switch keyboard for Codex
blurb: OpenAI released its first hardware, the Codex Micro, a $230 13-switch mechanical keyboard for monitoring and controlling Codex agents.

title: Apple and Google ordered to purge ‘nudify’ apps from App Stores
blurb: San Francisco ordered Apple and Google to remove dozens of "nudify" apps that use AI to generate non-consensual deepfake nudes from their app stores, citing California laws that criminalize facilitating such content. The city says both companies have profited from the apps for nearly a year despite repeated warnings, and gave them 28 days to respond or face civil penalties.

title: Meta is reportedly considering a multibillion-dollar data center deal with Anthropic
blurb: Meta is in early talks to lease data center capacity to Anthropic in a deal that could reach $10B over two years, according to The New York Times. The arrangement would open a cloud business for Meta, which earns almost all its revenue from advertising, and help fund the $125B to $145B it expects to spend on AI infrastructure in 2026.

title: Sources: Valar Atomics, which makes small nuclear reactors intended to power data centers, is in talks to raise $1B at a ~$5B pre-money valuation (The Information)
blurb: Valar Atomics, a startup building small nuclear reactors to power data centers, is in talks to raise $1B at a roughly $5B pre-money valuation, according to The Information. The round underscores how surging AI compute demand is pulling investor money into next-generation nuclear, as operators look for firm, carbon-free power that the grid can't supply fast enough.

title: Sources: AI inference chip startup Etched is raising funds at a ~$20B valuation and is raising capital at a $10B valuation in a separate round led by Sequoia (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Etched, an AI inference chip startup, is raising capital at a roughly $20B valuation, according to the Wall Street Journal, alongside a separate round at a $10B valuation led by Sequoia. The dueling figures underscore how much money is chasing custom inference silicon as buyers look for cheaper alternatives to Nvidia GPUs for running large models.

title: SpaceX in Talks to Sell Computing Power to Pentagon, WSJ Says
blurb: SpaceX is in talks to sell computing power to the US Department of Defense, the Wall Street Journal reported Friday. The deal would add to a widening set of infrastructure contracts for Elon Musk's rocket, satellite, and AI conglomerate, extending its reach from launch and connectivity into government compute.

title: China's BrainCo unveils what it says is the world's first integrated "brain-to-robot" platform that lets users control robots using an EEG headset (Minxiao Chang/South China Morning Post)
blurb: China's BrainCo introduced what it calls the first integrated "brain-to-robot" platform, letting users control robots through a non-invasive EEG headset that reads neural signals. The system pairs the headset with BrainCo's robotics stack to translate brain activity into commands, extending the company's earlier work on prosthetics and consumer neural interfaces toward direct machine control.

title: Sources: Apple and the DOJ are in early discussions about settling a 2024 antitrust lawsuit alleging that Apple violated antitrust laws (Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple and the DOJ have entered early talks to settle the government's 2024 antitrust lawsuit accusing the company of illegally monopolizing the smartphone market. A settlement would let Apple avoid a protracted court fight over claims that it locked users into its ecosystem, though sources caution the discussions remain preliminary and could still collapse.

title: The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index sank ~10% for the week, its largest weekly fall in over a year, and is now down ~20% from its late-June all-time high (Reuters)
blurb: The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell roughly 10% for the week, its steepest weekly drop in over a year, and now sits about 20% below its late-June record high. The selloff pushes the chip sector into correction territory, signaling that investor enthusiasm for AI-driven semiconductor demand has cooled sharply after months of gains.

title: The Investors With a New Way to Win in Silicon Valley
blurb: Venture firms are adjusting their playbooks to capture returns in a rare IPO boom, using tactics like concentrated late-stage bets on companies such as Anthropic rather than spreading capital across broad portfolios. The article profiles Spark Capital's Yasmin Razavi and others whose willingness to write large checks into a handful of AI leaders is redefining how Silicon Valley investors compete for the biggest exits.

title: Short sellers notch $8.7B profit as SpaceX shares dip to IPO price
blurb: Short sellers booked $8.7B in profit after SpaceX shares slipped below their IPO price, per Ortex data.

title: Amazon apologizes after some AWS users received bills as high as $1.5T due to "an issue with unit pricing within the estimated billing computation subsystem" (Robert Booth/The Guardian)
blurb: Amazon apologized after a billing subsystem glitch charged some AWS users bills as high as $1.5T.

title: Departing AWS exec Dave Brown is reportedly joining Meta, as Facebook parent mulls its own cloud
blurb: Dave Brown, the AWS executive who led its compute and AI services, is reportedly joining Meta in the coming weeks to work on its data center buildout. The hire comes as Meta weighs turning its AI infrastructure into a cloud business, a move CEO Mark Zuckerberg has floated and one that would pit Meta against AWS, which it still relies on.

title: NASA terminates Draper lunar lander mission
blurb: NASA and Draper terminated their $73M CP-12 lunar lander task order under the Commercial Lunar Payload Services program, citing redesign delays that pushed the landing to 2030 or 2031. Draper had been paid $43M for completed milestones, and NASA says it will still seek to deliver the mission's three far-side science payloads, including the Farside Seismic Suite and a heat-flow experiment.

title: China’s Moonshot AI Unveils Kimi Model, Threatening America’s Lead
blurb: Moonshot AI released Kimi, a freely available model from the Chinese lab that narrows the gap with frontier systems from OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic on several benchmarks. The open release adds to a wave of capable Chinese models undercutting US labs on price and access, intensifying questions about how durable America's lead in frontier AI really is.

title: Apple’s plot to crush OpenAI
blurb: The Vergecast breaks down Apple's lawsuit against OpenAI, weighing whether Apple fears a rival or is exploiting a weak moment.

title: Agility Robotics plants its flag in Tesla’s backyard
blurb: Agility Robotics is opening a 60,000-square-foot facility in Fremont, California to train its Digit humanoid robots, minutes from where Tesla plans to build Optimus. Unlike Tesla, Agility already ships a revenue-generating robot that hauls totes and bins for Amazon, GXO, and Toyota, and says it has secured $300M in contract orders ahead of a reverse merger that would make it the first pure-play humanoid firm to go public.

title: Apple ordered to remove 8 AI nonconsensual undressing apps from the App Store [U]
blurb: San Francisco city attorney David Chiu sent cease-and-desist letters ordering Apple and Google to pull 13 "nudify" apps that generate nonconsensual sexual deepfakes, eight from the App Store and five from the Play Store. Chiu argues the platforms have made millions in fees from the apps under California laws that prohibit supporting services that create deepfake pornography, renewing scrutiny that began with a Tech Transparency Project report six months ago.

title: AI-driven memory crunch jolts India’s smartphone market
blurb: AI data centers' appetite for memory chips has begun raising smartphone prices, and India offers the clearest evidence yet. Its shipments fell 10% year-over-year in Q2, the steepest June-quarter drop in six years, as manufacturers like Samsung and SK Hynix divert capacity toward more profitable high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerators, squeezing the sub-$210 handsets that make up 60% of the market.

title: AI Upheaval Crushes Retail Traders Crowding Into Leveraged Funds
blurb: A breakthrough from Chinese AI startup Moonshot sent semiconductor and AI stocks sharply lower Friday, echoing last year's DeepSeek selloff and hammering retail traders crowded into leveraged single-stock funds. The episode underscores how quickly market assumptions about AI winners can unravel, leaving concentrated, high-leverage bets especially exposed when a single research surprise reprices the sector.

title: SpaceX stock has cratered nearly 23% since the company joined the Nasdaq-100
blurb: SpaceX stock has fallen nearly 23% since joining the Nasdaq-100, sliding a sixth straight day after an aborted Starship test flight.

title: A bug in AWS has caused some customers' bills to spike from a few cents to billions of dollars
blurb: An AWS billing bug spiked some customers' estimated bills to millions or billions of dollars, including one $4.2 trillion charge.

title: Sources: SpaceX is in talks with the DOD about providing the agency with access to data-center capacity worth billions of dollars for running AI models (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: SpaceX is in talks with the Department of Defense about providing billions of dollars worth of data-center capacity for running AI models. The discussions would extend SpaceX's government business beyond satellite and launch services into AI compute infrastructure for military applications.

title: The White House is dictating access to frontier AI models, shifting power from tech giants, sources say
blurb: The Trump administration has begun dictating which companies and agencies can access frontier AI models, taking a decision that previously belonged to Anthropic and OpenAI, sources told CNBC. The shift follows last month's temporary block on Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5 over national security concerns, and comes as China's Moonshot AI released Kimi K3, which matches or beats US frontier models on at least one benchmark.

title: New NadMesh Botnet Hunts Exposed AI Services for Cloud Keys and Kubernetes Tokens
blurb: XLab researchers documented NadMesh, a Go botnet that uses a Shodan harvester to find exposed AI services like ComfyUI, Ollama, n8n, and Open WebUI, then strips cloud keys, Kubernetes service-account tokens, and MCP tool access from the machines behind them. MCP tops the operator's exploitation priority list via a JSON-RPC tools/call to execute_command, and XLab's sensors show distinct attacking IPs jumping from near zero to roughly 139 a day in early July.

title: Kimi K3, and what we can still learn from the pelican benchmark
blurb: Moonshot AI released Kimi K3, a 2.8-trillion-parameter model it calls the first "open 3T-class" system, with open weights promised by July 27. Self-reported and Artificial Analysis benchmarks put it ahead of Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 while trailing Claude Fable 5, and it now leads Arena.ai's Frontend Code arena. At $3/$15 per million tokens it is the most expensive model a Chinese lab has shipped, roughly triple Kimi K2.6's pricing.

title: Capital One releases VulnHunter, an open-source AI tool that finds software flaws before hackers do
blurb: Capital One released VulnHunter, an open-source agentic security tool that scans source code from an attacker's entry points forward to determine whether an exploit path survives existing defenses. A built-in falsification engine tries to disprove each finding before it reaches a human reviewer, cutting false positives, and surviving findings ship with an explained exploit path and a proposed fix. The Apache 2.0 tool runs on Claude Opus 4.8 inside Claude Code.

title: Anthropic, Meta reportedly discussing $10B data center leasing deal
blurb: Anthropic is reportedly in early talks to lease Meta's data center capacity in a deal that could be worth $10B over two years. The New York Times reported that Anthropic wants an early-cancellation clause similar to its SpaceX contract, under which it pays $1.25B per month for Colossus supercomputers; the Meta lease would run roughly $416M per month.

title: Sources: the Trump administration is considering plans for an independent regulator to vet the safety of AI models; the regulator would report to the SEC (Bloomberg)
blurb: The Trump administration is considering plans for an independent regulator to vet the safety of AI models before release, according to sources. The regulator would report to the SEC and represents a shift toward formal federal oversight of frontier model development.

title: AI spam filters are getting suckered by old-school text salting
blurb: Spammers are defeating AI-powered email filters with text salting, an old technique that inserts hidden or junk characters to corrupt how models parse a message. The tactic exploits the same tokenization and content-analysis steps that make LLM-based filters effective, letting malicious emails slip through while still rendering normally to human recipients.

title: Overtraining as the path to human-like AI
blurb: Gwern published a 13,000-word essay, Human-like Neural Nets by Catapulting, arguing that overtraining is the route to flexibly intelligent LLMs and explaining why current models fall short. The author, noting Gwern's early record on scaling, connects the argument to grokking, the effect where training a model well past apparent convergence triggers a sudden jump in capability as regularization forces it toward simpler, more general solutions.

title: What Is Moonshot AI? Why China’s New Model Is Roiling Markets
blurb: Chinese startup Moonshot AI released a new model on July 17 that drew global attention for its capabilities and sent stock markets falling worldwide. The launch renewed concerns about how quickly Chinese labs are closing the gap with US frontier models, echoing the market reaction to earlier low-cost releases from the country.

title: Databricks hits $188B valuation, extending its run as AI’s favorite second act
blurb: Databricks announced a new funding round led by Coatue that values the company at $188B, reportedly raising roughly $3B, though the money has not yet closed. The round extends an 18-month fundraising run that has repositioned Databricks as an AI provider, following a $134B valuation in February and products like Lakebase, its database for AI agents, and the Omnigent multi-agent harness.

title: How Apple’s big lawsuit could disrupt OpenAI’s IPO plans
blurb: Apple filed a trade secrets lawsuit against OpenAI, alleging a pattern of misconduct that reaches its chief hardware officer and claiming more than 400 former Apple employees now work there. The timing complicates OpenAI's hardware ambitions and a reported IPO that could come as early as later this year.

title: Brex built its AI agent policy by watching what agents actually do, not by writing rules first
blurb: Brex released CrabTrap, an open-source HTTP/HTTPS proxy that intercepts all agent network traffic and uses an LLM-as-a-judge to approve or deny each request against policy rules. The company derived its agent governance policy from observing real in-the-wild agent behavior rather than writing rules upfront, arguing enforcement should move from SDK-level permissions and model guardrails to a centralized network control plane.

title: Sources: AI inference chip startup Etched is raising funds at a ~$20B valuation and is raising capital at a $10B valuation in a separate round led by Sequoia (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Etched, an AI inference chip startup building specialized transformer hardware, is raising capital across two separate rounds, one at roughly a $20B valuation and another at a $10B valuation led by Sequoia. The financing would sharply mark up the company as it competes to challenge NVIDIA in the market for dedicated inference silicon.

title: Sources: OpenRouter has discussed a potential sale to a bigger tech company that could value it at billions of dollars, a premium to its $1.3B valuation in May (The Information)
blurb: OpenRouter has discussed a potential sale to a larger tech company at a price that could value it in the billions of dollars, according to The Information. The figure would mark a premium over the $1.3B valuation the AI model-routing startup reached in a funding round this past May.

title: Meta is reportedly considering a multibillion-dollar data center deal with Anthropic
blurb: Meta is reportedly in early talks to lease data center capacity to Anthropic in a deal that could reach $10B over two years, according to The New York Times. The arrangement would push Meta, which earns most of its revenue from advertising, into the cloud services business and turn its heavy AI infrastructure spending into a new revenue line.

title: Guide to AI Tokenomics: Eleven Principles for Token Efficient Software Engineering
blurb: This Google Cloud post lays out eleven principles for token-efficient software engineering with AI coding assistants, arguing that context bloat raises latency, cost, and hallucination rates while pulling human attention off real problems. The recommendations include starting with a balanced default model, packaging reusable SKILL.md skills, delegating output-heavy work to sub-agents, and shifting verification left so expensive browser smoke-tests run only right before handoff.

title: Swarming Claude Code and Codex in Parallel: Running Multiple Agents at Once with tmux and Git Worktrees
blurb: This post walks through a three-layer protocol for running multiple Codex and Claude Code agents in parallel, using a plan.json declaration that orchestrate-worktrees.js expands into a tmux session with isolated git worktrees. Separating each worker onto its own branch reduces merge-conflict risk to zero, letting tasks run simultaneously instead of serially blocking on each other and consolidating artifacts into per-worker files.

title: Chip Stocks Slide Into Bear Market in AI Unwind: Markets Wrap
blurb: Chipmaker stocks fell into a bear market as a deepening selloff dragged the sector down more than 20% from its recent peak. The decline reflected growing investor doubt that the pace of AI infrastructure spending can be justified by near-term returns, pressuring the group that had led the market's gains.

title: Gauge of Oracle’s Credit Risk Hits Fresh All Time High
blurb: A gauge of Oracle's credit risk hit a record high on Friday as investors weighed the company's aggressive cash-burn against uncertain returns on its AI infrastructure spending. The pressure intensified alongside the release of a new Chinese model positioned to compete with offerings from OpenAI and other frontier labs.

title: Meet the Companies Shelling Out for Top AI Models
blurb: Some companies are paying steep and rising prices for frontier AI models rather than switching to cheaper alternatives. The bet is that the performance gap on complex tasks justifies the premium, keeping demand for top-tier systems strong even as budgets climb.

title: China’s Moonshot AI Unveils Kimi Model, Threatening America’s Lead
blurb: Moonshot AI, a Chinese startup, released a freely available Kimi model that narrowed the performance gap with frontier systems from U.S. labs like OpenAI and Google. The open release adds to a growing lineup of competitive Chinese models and intensifies questions about how long American companies can maintain a lead at the top of the field.

title: Inference cloud operator General Compute raises $400M in debt financing
blurb: General Compute, an inference cloud operator, raised $400M in debt financing from Upper90, which will initially provide $100M and release more as customer demand grows. The company runs inference on AMD MI300X chips for the prefill phase and SambaNova accelerators for decoding, avoiding Nvidia GPUs and the liquid cooling most competitors require.

title: Perplexity launches SPACE runtime for AI agent tasks
blurb: Perplexity launched SPACE, short for Sandboxed Platform for Agentic Code Execution, a runtime that supports long-running agents executing code, editing files, and completing multi-step tasks across VM-isolated sandboxes. It now powers 100% of Perplexity Computer sessions, and a btrfs storage layer with warm pools cut median sandbox creation time from 185ms to 60ms.

title: Intuit scrapped its own AI agent architecture twice in four months. At VB Transform 2026, its AI VP called that the fast path
blurb: Intuit rebuilt its production AI agent architecture twice in about four months, and VP of AI Nhung Ho argued at VB Transform 2026 that scrapping the work was the fast path. The company moved from specialist agents to a central orchestration layer, then abandoned that layer when agents passing results in natural language compounded errors at every handoff across a ten-agent chain. The second rebuild took 60 days, with a working version in under 20, and won engineering buy-in by demoing the new system against real production queries.

title: AI Upheaval Crushes Retail Traders Crowding Into Leveraged Funds
blurb: A breakthrough from Chinese startup Moonshot sent AI and semiconductor stocks sharply lower on Friday, echoing last year's DeepSeek selloff and hitting retail traders concentrated in leveraged funds. The drop underscored how quickly market assumptions about AI winners can reverse, leaving crowded single-stock and leveraged positions exposed to sudden downside.

title: US Stocks End Week Lower on Fears of Another ‘DeepSeek Moment’
blurb: US stocks closed the week lower as chipmakers sold off following the release of a new AI model from a Chinese startup. Investors drew comparisons to the original "DeepSeek moment," when a cheaper-than-expected Chinese model triggered a sharp repricing of US semiconductor and AI-infrastructure names.

title: OpenAI unveils GPT-Red to boost AI safety with internal red-teaming
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-Red, an internal-only automated red-teaming model trained via self-play reinforcement learning to find prompt-injection vulnerabilities before deployment. It attacked GPT-5.1 in 84% of unseen scenarios versus 13% for human red-teamers, and its attacks have been folded into production training since GPT-5.3, cutting GPT-5.6 Sol's direct prompt-injection failure rate to 0.05%.

title: Compute Exchange opens secondary market for used Nvidia H100 and A100 GPUs
blurb: Compute Exchange launched a secondary marketplace for used and refurbished Nvidia H100 and A100 GPUs, matching buyers with qualified suppliers and handling sourcing, logistics, and transaction execution. Requests have ranged from hundreds to tens of thousands of GPUs, with demand concentrated on proven production hardware rather than newer Blackwell systems, as buyers chase cheaper capacity for workloads that don't require the latest chips.

title: Sources: SpaceX is in talks with the DOD about providing the agency with access to data-center capacity worth billions of dollars for running AI models (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: SpaceX is in talks with the Department of Defense to supply the agency with billions of dollars of data-center capacity for running AI models, according to the Wall Street Journal. The move would push Elon Musk's company past rockets and satellites into government AI infrastructure, a market where hyperscalers like Microsoft and Amazon have set the terms. For founders, it signals that compute capacity itself is becoming the contested asset, and the buyers with the deepest pockets are increasingly governments, not enterprises.

title: The White House is dictating access to frontier AI models, shifting power from tech giants, sources say
blurb: The Trump administration is now deciding which companies get access to frontier AI models, a call that until now belonged to Anthropic and OpenAI, sources told CNBC. Both labs already gate their most powerful releases, and last month the White House blocked Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5 over national security concerns before reinstating access after weeks of negotiation. The timing is the story: Chinese startup Moonshot just shipped Kimi K3, which matches Fable and GPT-5.6 and beats them on at least one benchmark, so Washington is tightening control exactly as its lead narrows.

title: Sources: the Trump administration is considering plans for an independent regulator to vet the safety of AI models; the regulator would report to the SEC (Bloomberg)
blurb: The Trump administration is weighing a new independent regulator to vet the safety of AI models before release, with the body reporting to the SEC. Housing AI oversight under the securities regulator ties model safety to public-market disclosure and investor protection rather than a standalone tech agency. For founders shipping frontier or high-risk models, a pre-release safety review would add a compliance gate that favors labs with the legal and testing budgets to clear it.

title: What Is Moonshot AI? Why China’s New Model Is Roiling Markets
blurb: Chinese startup Moonshot AI released a new model on July 17 that matched frontier performance and knocked stock markets down around the world. The pattern rhymes with the DeepSeek shock: a lesser-known Chinese lab ships something competitive with the US labs, and investors reprice the assumption that American compute spending buys a durable lead. For founders, the read is that model quality is commoditizing faster than the capex narrative admits, and the moat is moving up the stack toward data, workflow, and distribution rather than the raw model.

title: Databricks hits $188B valuation, extending its run as AI’s favorite second act
blurb: Databricks raised a round led by Coatue that values it at $188B, roughly $3B on top of a $134B valuation set just five months ago in February. The number that matters is the pace: four rounds in 18 months carried it from $62B to $188B, funded by a clean rebrand from big-data SaaS into an AI infrastructure provider selling the governance and security enterprises already expect. The company is also one of the loudest enterprise champions of cheaper Chinese open-weight models like Z.ai's GLM 5.2, betting cost control is where the next wave of adoption gets decided.

title: How Apple’s big lawsuit could disrupt OpenAI’s IPO plans
blurb: Apple filed a trade secrets lawsuit against OpenAI last Friday, alleging misconduct that reaches its chief hardware officer and claiming more than 400 former Apple employees now work there. The timing lands hard, with OpenAI reportedly eyeing an IPO as early as this year and building its own hardware ambitions on the exact talent Apple says it poached. A drawn-out fight over trade secrets is the kind of overhang that spooks public-market investors and can push a listing window out by quarters.

title: Sources: OpenRouter has discussed a potential sale to a bigger tech company that could value it at billions of dollars, a premium to its $1.3B valuation in May (The Information)
blurb: OpenRouter has held talks about selling to a larger tech company at a valuation in the billions, a step up from the $1.3B it commanded in May. The startup routes API traffic across hundreds of models, sitting in the middle of the fast-growing market for AI inference. A sale so soon after a fresh round signals that the labs and infrastructure players want to own the routing layer rather than compete with it, and that operators building on aggregators may find their supplier absorbed into a bigger stack.

title: Sources: Valar Atomics, which makes small nuclear reactors intended to power data centers, is in talks to raise $1B at a ~$5B pre-money valuation (The Information)
blurb: Valar Atomics is in talks to raise $1B at a roughly $5B pre-money valuation to build small nuclear reactors aimed at powering data centers. The round marks how fast AI compute demand is pulling capital into power generation, with investors now underwriting reactor startups at multiples once reserved for software. The bottleneck for the next wave of data centers is increasingly electricity, not chips, and the money is moving to match.

title: Sources: AI inference chip startup Etched is raising funds at a ~$20B valuation and is raising capital at a $10B valuation in a separate round led by Sequoia (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Etched, a startup building chips specialized for AI inference rather than training, is raising at a roughly $20B valuation, with a separate round led by Sequoia pegging it near $10B. The company bets that fixed-architecture silicon aimed only at running transformer models can beat Nvidia's general-purpose GPUs on cost and speed for the workloads that now dominate AI spend. Capital is moving from the training layer, where Nvidia is entrenched, toward inference, where the economics of every AI product get decided and a challenger still has room to win.

title: The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index sank ~10% for the week, its largest weekly fall in over a year, and is now down ~20% from its late-June all-time high (Reuters)
blurb: The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell about 10% for the week, its steepest weekly drop in more than a year, and now sits roughly 20% below its late-June record high. The pullback hits the trade that has carried the market, as chipmakers riding AI compute demand give back a chunk of the gains that made them the year's momentum leaders. For founders, it signals that the capital funding the AI buildout is no longer priced as a one-way bet.

title: Nuclear startup Valar Atomics in talks to raise new funding at $6B valuation
blurb: Valar Atomics is in talks to raise a $1B round at a $6B valuation, with Sequoia expected to lead, just months after raising $450M at a $2B valuation. The three-year-old startup builds small modular reactors and recently powered an Nvidia AI chip in a proof-of-concept, alongside a partnership to explore nuclear power for data centers. Utilities are years behind on the electricity that AI compute demands, and that vacuum is what's pulling capital into a nuclear sector long defined by cost overruns and regulatory delays.

title: SpaceX in Talks to Sell Computing Power to Pentagon, WSJ Says
blurb: SpaceX is in talks to sell computing power to the Pentagon, per the WSJ, another infrastructure deal for Musk's rocket, satellite, and AI conglomerate. The pattern to watch is a launch and satellite company converting its capital base into a defense compute vendor, competing for the same government AI budgets as the hyperscalers. For founders, it signals that compute supply is consolidating into a few vertically integrated players who own the power, the orbit, and now the inference contracts.

title: Chip Stocks Slide Into Bear Market in AI Unwind: Markets Wrap
blurb: Chipmaker stocks have fallen into a bear market, down more than 20% as investors start to doubt that the AI spending boom can keep justifying the multiples. The trigger is a growing sense that hyperscaler capex is running ahead of the revenue it produces, and the semiconductor names that led the rally on the way up are now leading it down. For founders raising or selling into AI, the cheap-capital tailwind that funded aggressive infrastructure bets is thinning, and budgets built on it will get scrutinized harder.

title: Meet the Companies Shelling Out for Top AI Models
blurb: Some companies are paying up for frontier AI models over cheaper alternatives, even as prices keep climbing. The logic is that the most capable systems clear tasks the smaller models still fumble, so the higher token cost buys reliability instead of rework. That splits the market into two camps: teams routing everything to the cheapest model that gets it right, and teams treating frontier access as a line item worth defending because the accuracy gap decides whether the workflow ships at all.

title: Gauge of Oracle’s Credit Risk Hits Fresh All Time High
blurb: Oracle's credit-default swaps hit a record high on Friday as investors questioned whether its aggressive AI spending will ever pay off. The company is burning cash to build data-center capacity on the bet that AI demand keeps compounding, but a new Chinese model that rivals OpenAI's offerings just made that payoff look less certain. When the market starts pricing default risk into the balance sheet of a name this size, it is signaling doubt about the entire debt-funded AI buildout, not just one vendor.

title: The Investors With a New Way to Win in Silicon Valley
blurb: A new class of venture investors is winning by abandoning the spray-and-pray model and concentrating enormous checks into a handful of AI leaders like Anthropic. The bet is that in this IPO boom, a few companies capture most of the returns, so ownership in the winners matters more than the number of names in the portfolio. For founders, it means capital is pooling around presumed category leaders, and if you aren't one of the anointed few, the terms you get look very different from the ones filling the headlines.

title: Intuit scrapped its own AI agent architecture twice in four months. At VB Transform 2026, its AI VP called that the fast path
blurb: Intuit rebuilt its production AI agent architecture twice in four months, and its VP of AI called that scrapping the fast path. The orchestration layer that routed tasks across ten specialist agents broke by design, because agents passed results to each other in natural language and every handoff compounded error, so the team rebuilt on a skills-and-tools system in 60 days. The move that won engineering buy-in was evidence, not argument: a demo built on real production queries that beat the existing system on the same tasks.

title: Inference cloud operator General Compute raises $400M in debt financing
blurb: General Compute raised $400M in debt financing from Upper90, starting with a $100M draw and pulling more as customer demand grows. What sets it apart is the hardware: instead of Nvidia, it runs prefill on AMD's MI300X and decoding on SambaNova chips, both of which fit standard air-cooled racks and skip the expensive liquid cooling most GPU clusters need. The bet is that a specialized two-chip inference stack can add capacity faster and cheaper than the Nvidia default, and debt rather than equity signals a business already selling steady compute.

title: Departing AWS exec Dave Brown is reportedly joining Meta, as Facebook parent mulls its own cloud
blurb: AWS lost the executive who ran its compute and AI services, Dave Brown, to Meta, where he will help build out the data centers behind a possible cloud business of its own. Meta hasn't committed to selling cloud, but Zuckerberg has said the demand is there, with companies already asking to pay for access to its models and spare capacity. With $125B to $145B in capex going into AI data centers this year, Meta turning that buildout into a rival cloud would put it in direct competition with AWS, the supplier it now depends on.

title: The Week’s 10 Biggest Funding Rounds: No Summer Doldrums As Dollars Still Flow To AI
blurb: AI money didn't take a summer break, with the week's top 10 US rounds led by a $1.5B Series D for Fireworks AI at a $17.5B valuation. AI drug-discovery startup Chai Discovery took $400M at $3.8B, and Walden Robotics came out of stealth with $300M and a $1.1B valuation, so the capital is spreading past pure software into life sciences, robotics, drones, and construction automation. The mix says investors are still writing nine-figure checks against AI applied to physical and regulated industries, not just model tooling.

title: Learning a few things about running SQLite
blurb: Julia Evans documents operational lessons from running SQLite in production behind a Django ORM, starting with a FTS5 query on a 4000-row table that dropped from 5 seconds to 0.05 seconds after running ANALYZE to give the query planner real statistics. She also covers how slow bulk DELETE statements collide with the 5-second busy timeout, crashing concurrent writer workers when a cleanup transaction holds the database too long.

title: Swarming Claude Code and Codex in Parallel: Running Multiple Agents at Once with tmux and Git Worktrees
blurb: The author runs multiple Claude Code and Codex agents in parallel using a three-layer protocol, where a plan.json declares workers that an orchestrator script expands into isolated git worktrees launched inside a tmux session. Separating each agent onto its own branch drops merge-conflict risk to zero and removes the serial bottleneck where one job's commits change the preconditions for the next, at the cost of managing seed-path copying and artifact consolidation across sessions.

title: Guide to AI Tokenomics: Eleven Principles for Token Efficient Software Engineering
blurb: Google Cloud engineers lay out eleven principles for token-efficient AI coding, arguing that context bloat is the main driver of latency, hallucination, and cost as agents write more of the code. Their tactics include starting with a balanced default model, packaging reusable skills in SKILL.md files, delegating output-heavy work to sub-agents, and checkpointing often so sessions can restart from a clean, low-token state.

title: Giving an AI assistant read-only access to Microsoft Loop — without breaking permissions
blurb: loop-reader-mcp is an open-source (MIT) MCP server that gives an AI assistant read-only access to Microsoft Loop pages, which have no content API and store data in SharePoint Embedded containers that only accept app-only tokens. It avoids permission-flattening by splitting discovery from retrieval, running security-trimmed Graph Search under the user's delegated token via OAuth On-Behalf-Of and using the app identity only to download content the user can already see.

title: Seven Malicious Vite npm Packages Use Blockchain C2 to Deliver a RAT
blurb: Seven malicious npm packages typosquat the @vitejs/* namespace, delivering a RAT via blockchain-based C2 that resists takedown.

title: Meta’s Spark Muse 1.1 is now available on Databricks, fully governed by Unity AI Gateway
blurb: Databricks added Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 to Unity AI Gateway through new Model Provider Services, letting teams register providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Bedrock once in Unity Catalog and query any model on day one under existing permissions. The pitch to platform teams is centralized governance over model sprawl, with encrypted credentials, per-team rate limits, and automatic tracking of token usage, latency, and cost attribution across every provider.

title: Learning a few things about running SQLite
blurb: Julia Evans documents operating SQLite as a real production database for a Django site, learning that "SQLite is fine for small sites" glosses over the operational work databases still demand. Running ANALYZE cut a 4000-row FTS5 query from 5 seconds to 0.05 by giving the planner statistics to work with. The sharper lesson is concurrency: a slow bulk DELETE held a write lock long enough for another worker to hit its 5-second timeout and crash the VM.

title: Is "jobs" a four-letter word?
blurb: The author argues that headcount was once Silicon Valley's default scoreboard for ambition, with founders measuring success by how many people they planned to hire rather than by revenue or product. That norm has quietly inverted, and the piece traces how "jobs" became something startups now brag about avoiding, with hiring reframed as a cost to minimize rather than a signal of momentum.

title: Capital One releases VulnHunter, an open-source AI tool that finds software flaws before hackers do
blurb: Capital One released VulnHunter, an open-source (Apache 2.0) agentic tool that scans source code using "attacker-first forward analysis," reasoning from real entry points like APIs and file uploads to confirm exploit paths survive existing defenses. A built-in falsification engine tries to disprove each finding before it reaches a reviewer, cutting the false positives that plague reverse scanners; it currently runs on Claude Opus 4.8 inside Claude Code.

title: Inc Ransomware Exploits SonicWall SMA Zero-Days
blurb: Inc ransomware is exploiting two SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days, CVE-2026-15409 (unauthenticated SSRF, CVSS 10.0) and CVE-2026-15410 (authenticated command injection, CVSS 7.2), chaining them for unauthenticated RCE and root-level command execution to harvest credentials ahead of ransomware deployment. SonicWall published an advisory on July 14 and CISA added both to its KEV catalog, so patch SMA 1000 appliances immediately and rotate exposed credentials, as a PoC for the SSRF flaw is already public.

title: HollowByte DDoS flaw bloats OpenSSL server memory with 11-byte payload
blurb: HollowByte, a DoS flaw disclosed by Okta's Red Team, lets an unauthenticated attacker send an 11-byte TLS handshake message with an oversized length field, forcing vulnerable OpenSSL versions to allocate the declared buffer before validating the payload and leaving worker threads blocked on data that never arrives. Repeated connections fragment the heap so RSS stays bloated until restart; OpenSSL silently patched and backported the fix with no identifier, so upgrade the embedded library across NGINX, Apache, and language runtimes.

title: Seven Malicious Vite npm Packages Use Blockchain C2 to Deliver a RAT
blurb: Checkmarx disclosed ViteVenom, a cluster of seven malicious npm packages using scoped names to impersonate the "@vitejs/*" namespace and deliver a RAT capable of reverse shells, credential harvesting, file exfiltration, and backdoor injection. Attributed to threat actor SuccessKey, the loader executes at import rather than install time to evade endpoint detection and pulls later stages from a four-tier blockchain C2 spanning Tron, Aptos, and Binance Smart Chain that resists takedown.

title: New wp2shell WordPress Core Flaw Lets Unauthenticated Attackers Run Code
blurb: WordPress shipped 6.9.5 and 7.0.2 on July 17 to close a pre-auth RCE in core (branded wp2shell by Assetnote's Adam Kues) that an anonymous HTTP request can trigger against a default install with no plugins, affecting 6.9.0 through 6.9.4 and 7.0.0 through 7.0.1. No CVE or CVSS has been assigned yet, so verify your running version directly rather than trusting the forced auto-update to have landed.

title: GoldenEyeDog Subgroup Linked to DigiCert Breach and Code-Signing Certificate Theft
blurb: Expel attributed the April 2026 DigiCert breach to CylindricalCanine, a subgroup of the Chinese cybercrime group GoldenEyeDog (APT-Q-27, Dragon Breath), which used a modified Gh0st RAT variant called Golden Gh0st RAT to compromise a support employee's device and steal code-signing certificates intended for DigiCert customers. Organizations that received DigiCert certificates in that window should treat them as potentially compromised, monitor for binaries signed with stolen certs, and prioritize revocation and reissuance.

title: Abbott probes two cyber incidents amid extortion claims
blurb: Abbott Laboratories is investigating two cyber incidents, including confirmed unauthorized access to legacy Exact Sciences systems in its Cancer Diagnostics business after the ShinyHunters extortion gang listed the company on its leak site. ShinyHunters claims it breached a Microsoft Entra SSO account via a mid-June vishing campaign against employees, part of an ongoing push against medtech firms including Medtronic, iRhythm, and Stryker.

title: New NadMesh Botnet Hunts Exposed AI Services for Cloud Keys and Kubernetes Tokens
blurb: NadMesh, a Go botnet detailed by QiAnXin's XLab, scans Shodan for exposed AI services like ComfyUI, Ollama, n8n, and Open WebUI, then steals cloud keys from environment variables, k8s service-account tokens, and files like ~/.aws/config and .env. It prioritizes unauthenticated MCP servers, using a JSON-RPC tools/call to execute_command, and distinct source IPs pushing it climbed from near zero in late June to roughly 139 a day.

title: AI spam filters are getting suckered by old-school text salting
blurb: Researchers found that text salting, an old trick of padding messages with hidden or junk characters, reliably evades modern AI and ML-based spam filters that classify on semantic content. Attackers insert zero-width characters, invisible HTML, or off-screen text to poison the model's read of a message while leaving it legible to the victim, so defenders should normalize and strip hidden characters before classification rather than trusting the model alone.

title: Brex built its AI agent policy by watching what agents actually do, not by writing rules first
blurb: Brex open-sourced CrabTrap, an HTTP/HTTPS proxy that intercepts all agent network traffic and uses an LLM-as-a-judge to approve or deny each request against policy, rather than relying on scoped tokens or model guardrails that prompt injection can bypass. The company built its agent policies by observing real in-the-wild behavior instead of writing rules upfront. The takeaway for security teams: treat the network layer as a centralized enforcement point for agent governance, since every outbound request is an interceptable policy decision.

title: Show HN: Watch bots interact with an SSH honeypot in real time
blurb: A live dashboard streaming real-time SSH honeypot telemetry: source IPs, credential attempts, commands, and client fingerprints from inbound attacker connections.

title: OpenSSL HollowByte Flaw Could Freeze Server Memory with 11-Byte TLS Requests
blurb: OpenSSL's HollowByte flaw, disclosed by Okta's Red Team, lets 11-byte TLS handshake messages force an unpatched server to pre-allocate up to 131 KB per connection for a body that never arrives, and on glibc systems heap fragmentation keeps that memory frozen after the attacker disconnects. OpenSSL shipped fixes on June 9 (4.0.1, 3.6.3, 3.5.7, 3.4.6, 3.0.21) with no CVE or advisory, so upgrade to those releases rather than relying on connection limits.

title: Why Your AI Agent Has an Identity Problem
blurb: AI agents deployed to production typically run under shared service accounts with over-broad permissions, no distinct identity, and no audit trail that can reconstruct what they accessed or why. The author argues traditional IAM breaks because it assumes a human actor, while an agent may make 40 API calls in 30 seconds, touch 500 customer records for one task, and delegate to other agents no human approves. Provisioning per-agent identity early cuts both audit-failure risk and the cost of scaling agentic systems.

title: Cloudflare WAF protects WordPress applications from two high-severity vulnerabilities
blurb: Cloudflare deployed WAF rules for two WordPress flaws disclosed pre-release: CVE-2026-63030, an unauthenticated RCE reachable through the REST API batch endpoint when no persistent object cache is in use (Critical), and CVE-2026-60137, a related SQL injection (High). WordPress fixed both in 7.0.2 with backports to 6.9.5 and 6.8.6 and is forcing automatic updates, but the WAF rules only cover traffic proxied through Cloudflare and don't replace patching.

title: Levi’s builds toward unified ERP system by mid-2027
blurb: Levi Strauss migrated its Asia and Beyond Yoga operations onto the single platform it first deployed in North America, advancing a plan to run the entire company on one unified ERP system by mid-2027. Consolidating regional and subsidiary systems onto a common backbone aims to standardize supply-chain and financial data, though the multiyear timeline signals how much integration work remains across the apparel maker's global footprint.

title: HollowByte DDoS flaw bloats OpenSSL server memory with 11-byte payload
blurb: Okta's Red Team disclosed HollowByte, an unauthenticated OpenSSL flaw that lets an 11-byte TLS handshake payload force the server to allocate large memory buffers it never fills, permanently bloating resident memory until the process restarts. OpenSSL silently patched the bug with no CVE assigned and backported the fix to older releases, so IT teams running NGINX, Apache, Node.js, or PostgreSQL should prioritize upgrading the embedded library.

title: Abbott probes two cyber incidents amid extortion claims
blurb: Abbott Laboratories is investigating two cyber incidents, confirming unauthorized access to legacy Exact Sciences systems in its Cancer Diagnostics unit after the ShinyHunters extortion gang listed it on a data leak site. The breach traces to a vishing attack that compromised a Microsoft Entra SSO account, underscoring the SSO-and-SaaS attack path now hitting medtech vendors including Medtronic, Stryker, and iRhythm.

title: Inc Ransomware Exploits SonicWall SMA Zero-Days
blurb: SonicWall disclosed two zero-day flaws in its SMA 1000 Series appliances, CVE-2026-15409 (a maximum-severity 10.0 SSRF) and CVE-2026-15410 (a 7.2 command-injection bug), which an affiliate of the Inc ransomware group has chained to gain unauthenticated root access on enterprise networks. CISA added both to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, and with a public proof-of-concept already circulating, IT teams running SMA gateways should patch immediately.

title: Microsoft cuts OneDrive support for older Windows 10 versions next month
blurb: Microsoft will end OneDrive desktop app support for Windows 10 versions 1709 through 21H2 starting next month, pushing affected users onto newer builds or the web client to keep syncing. IT teams still running legacy Windows 10 endpoints face a forced upgrade cycle ahead of the OS's broader end-of-support deadline, adding another migration item to already crowded 2026 refresh plans.

title: New wp2shell WordPress Core Flaw Lets Unauthenticated Attackers Run Code
blurb: WordPress shipped 6.9.5 and 7.0.2 on July 17, closing a pre-auth RCE in core that an anonymous HTTP request can trigger against a default install with no plugins, affecting versions 6.9.0 through 7.0.1. WordPress enabled forced auto-updates to push the fix, but has not confirmed it reaches sites with auto-updates disabled, so admins should verify their running version rather than assume they are patched.

title: Google Workspace Weekly Recap - July 17, 2026
blurb: Google's latest Workspace recap adds FIDO2 physical security key support to Google Credential Provider for Windows, letting admins enforce hardware-key 2-Step Verification at the Windows login screen. The same update renames NotebookLM to Gemini Notebook, opens Google Chat group conversations to external collaborators, and extends Gemini in Docs to 11 more languages including Mandarin, Turkish, and Czech.

title: Learning a few things about running SQLite
blurb: Julia Evans documents operational lessons from running SQLite in production behind a Django site, starting with the discovery that a 4000-row FTS5 query dropped from 5 seconds to 0.05 seconds after running ANALYZE to give the query planner table statistics. The write-up also covers a failure mode where slow bulk DELETEs hold a transaction long enough that concurrent workers hit the 5-second busy timeout and crash, a reminder that "SQLite is fine for small sites" still means operating a real database.

title: Cloudflare WAF protects WordPress applications from two high-severity vulnerabilities
blurb: Cloudflare deployed WAF rules protecting all plan tiers, including free, against two high-severity WordPress flaws: an unauthenticated RCE in the REST API batch endpoint (CVE-2026-63030, critical) and a related SQL injection (CVE-2026-60137, high). The rules buy time but are not a substitute for patching, and WordPress is force-pushing automatic updates through fixes in 7.0.2, 6.9.5, and 6.8.6.

title: OpenSSL HollowByte Flaw Could Freeze Server Memory with 11-Byte TLS Requests
blurb: Okta's Red Team disclosed HollowByte, a denial-of-service flaw where an unpatched OpenSSL server allocates up to 131 KB per TLS handshake header before any body arrives, and glibc's chunk-caching keeps that memory frozen after the attacker disconnects. OpenSSL patched it quietly on June 9 with no CVE or advisory, so scanners can't flag it, and Okta's NGINX tests froze 25% of a 16 GB server without hitting connection limits.

title: FTX to distribute roughly $900 million to creditors in fifth wave of payouts
blurb: FTX's bankruptcy estate will distribute roughly $900 million to creditors in its fifth payout wave, bringing total repayments to nearly $10 billion since distributions began in 2025. The ongoing recovery continues to return capital to claimants of the collapsed exchange, with each wave clearing a larger share of the estate's outstanding obligations.

title: Ondo Finance Partners With SBI to Tokenize Japanese Assets
blurb: Ondo Finance partnered with SBI Group to tokenize Japanese real-world assets, with distribution across SBI's ecosystem and settlement in the group's yen-denominated JPYSC stablecoin. The deal is a path-to-market agreement rather than a launched product, but it sent ONDO up roughly 17% over 24 hours to about $0.39, near a $1.89B circulating market cap.

title: OKX Launches Tokenized US Stocks on Shared Order Book
blurb: OKX launched Unified Tokenized Stocks, listing more than 40 tokenized US equities and ETFs including XNVDA, XAAPL and XTSLA that trade against USDT around the clock, with US and EU users excluded. Each ticker is backed 1:1 by shares held in custody via Backed's xStocks and routes every issuer's version into a single order book, OKX's pitch against the fragmented per-issuer markets on Kraken and Binance.

title: Polymarket traders cut Clarity Act passage odds to record low as Senate delay drags on
blurb: Polymarket traders now give the CLARITY Act a 32% chance of passing by year-end, its lowest level since the market launched in January and down from an 82% peak in February. The slide reflects a narrowing Senate calendar and an unresolved bipartisan ethics provision, leaving crypto's federal market-structure framework and clearer SEC-CFTC jurisdictional lines stalled ahead of any 2026 vote.

title: AI Meets Cryptography 2: What AI Found in OpenVM's ZkVM
blurb: zkSecurity's second post in its AI-auditing series points its zkao agent at OpenVM's zkVM, where the tool surfaced a critical soundness bug in the openvm-pairing guest library that let a malicious prover forge any pairing equality. The flaw, assigned CVE-2026-46669 and fixed in OpenVM 1.6.0, affected only code using the vulnerable library, not the proving system itself. The writeup details why naive LLM setups fail on densely coupled zkVM code and how the team validated the AI's candidate finding before disclosure.

title: US Stocks End Week Lower on Fears of Another ‘DeepSeek Moment’
blurb: US stocks closed the week lower after a Chinese startup released a new AI model, reigniting the "DeepSeek moment" fears that a cheaper frontier model could undercut US chip demand. Semiconductor names led the decline, extending a selloff that has tied equity sentiment to every signal about AI training costs and capex assumptions.

title: Ondo Finance Partners With SBI to Tokenize Japanese Assets
blurb: Ondo Finance partnered with SBI Group to tokenize Japanese assets, distributing them across SBI's ecosystem and settling in the group's JPYSC yen stablecoin under Japan's stablecoin framework. The deal is a path to market rather than a launched product, positioning Ondo's real-world-asset tokenization inside one of Japan's largest financial-services firms as SBI builds a global corridor for digital assets.

title: Bank of America taps new leaders to bridge crypto, AI and traditional finance
blurb: Bank of America named Sonali Theisen head of its global digital assets platform and Kevin Milsom head of AI transformation, creating dedicated leadership to connect crypto, AI, and its traditional banking operations. The appointments signal that a top-four US bank is treating digital assets and AI as core infrastructure rather than experiments, adding pressure on peers to formalize their own digital-asset strategies.

title: Stripe and Swift race to control the next generation of global payments infrastructure
blurb: Swift said it will expand its blockchain-based settlement network to more than 40 financial institutions after piloting with 17 global banks, while Stripe made an unsolicited $53B bid for PayPal to combine its merchant network with one of the largest consumer wallets. The moves mark a shift from proving blockchain to controlling distribution, as stablecoins become core payments infrastructure and firms race to own wallets, merchant acceptance, and settlement layers.
